skill.name: memory-systems
skill.description: Design agent memory architectures for persistent state, entity tracking, temporal knowledge graphs, and retrieval across sessions.
skill.summary: Plan durable memory layers for agents that need reliable context beyond one session.
skill.author: ChakshuGautam

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported external command execution, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns. Review found no prompt injection, no real shell execution, and no system reconnaissance; command findings are markdown code fences, and most crypto hits are terminology false positives. The only confirmed issue is MD5 used for sample graph identifiers, which is low risk because it is not used for secrets or security decisions.

low_findings.title: MD5 Used for Non-Security Identifiers
low_findings.description: The sample Python implementation uses hashlib.md5 to generate shortened graph node and edge identifiers. This is not used for passwords, signatures, or secret material, so the impact is low, but uuid4 or secrets-based IDs would avoid collision concerns.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The weak algorithm is directly present in ID generation code. Context shows it creates internal graph IDs, not cryptographic protections or credential handling.

low_findings.title: External Command Findings Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported external command locations are fenced Python examples in markdown. No shell invocation, subprocess call, Ruby backtick execution, or command string construction appears in the cited sections.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited backticks are markdown fence delimiters for Python examples. The reviewed files do not contain executable shell command APIs at those locations.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Findings Are Benign Memory Logic
low_findings.description: The reported reconnaissance locations are ordinary graph, timestamp, and metadata operations inside memory examples. No hostname, user, process, environment, network, or platform enumeration behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited code stores graph labels, timestamps, and relationship metadata. I found no evidence of host reconnaissance or data collection about the runtime system.

dangerous_patterns.title: Weak Hash Algorithm in Example Code
dangerous_patterns.description: hashlib.md5 appears in executable sample code for ID generation. It should not be reused for security-sensitive identifiers, authentication, signatures, or integrity checks.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is present in code and uses a known weak hash. The low-impact context prevents a higher severity assessment.

content.value_statement: Agents often lose useful context when a session ends or facts change over time. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code design layered memory systems with retrieval, entities, and temporal validity.
actual_capabilities: Explains working, short-term, long-term, entity, and temporal memory layers.
Compares vector stores, metadata search, knowledge graphs, and temporal knowledge graphs.
Provides Python examples for vector search, graph storage, temporal queries, and consolidation.
Guides retrieval patterns for semantic, entity-based, and time-aware memory access.
Shows how to inject retrieved memories into an agent context within token limits.
limitations: The included embedding logic is a placeholder and not production quality.
The examples are in-memory prototypes and do not include durable database setup.
Privacy, retention, consent, and deletion policies must be designed separately.
The skill does not benchmark a live memory system or tune retrieval automatically.

use_cases.title: Build Persistent Agent Context
use_cases.description: Design memory layers so an assistant can recall preferences, prior decisions, and task state across sessions.

use_cases.title: Model Entity Relationships
use_cases.description: Use graph memory to track customers, projects, documents, and relationships that must remain consistent over time.

use_cases.title: Plan Temporal Knowledge Retrieval
use_cases.description: Create a time-aware memory design that can answer questions about facts valid during a specific period.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Memory Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose a memory architecture for an agent that needs to remember user preferences across sessions. Compare file storage, vector search, and graph memory.

prompt_templates.title: Design Entity Memory
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an entity memory layer for my agent. Include entity IDs, properties, relationship types, retrieval methods, and update rules.

prompt_templates.title: Add Temporal Validity
prompt_templates.prompt: Extend this memory design with temporal validity. Explain how facts should be stored, expired, queried by date, and consolidated.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Memory Implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my agent memory implementation for retrieval quality, stale facts, privacy risks, and scaling limits. Suggest concrete design changes.

output_examples.input: I need an agent to remember customer preferences and project history.
output_examples.output: A layered design with session memory for active work, long-term entity memory for customers, and graph relationships for projects and decisions.

output_examples.input: How should I handle facts that change over time?
output_examples.output: A temporal graph plan that stores valid-from and valid-until dates, queries facts at a target time, and retires outdated relationships.

output_examples.input: My vector memory returns related text but misses relationships.
output_examples.output: A migration path that adds entity extraction, relationship storage, metadata filters, and graph traversal for relationship-aware retrieval.

best_practices: Start with the simplest memory layer that supports the required query patterns.
Store source, timestamp, confidence, and retention metadata with each memory.
Review privacy, deletion, and consent requirements before persisting user data.
anti_patterns: Do not store every conversation turn forever without consolidation or retention rules.
Do not rely on vector similarity when relationship or time-aware reasoning is required.
Do not inject large memory dumps into context when targeted retrieval is enough.

faq.question: Does this skill provide a complete production database?
faq.answer: No. It provides architecture guidance and prototype examples that need production storage, monitoring, and security controls.

faq.question: Can it help choose between vector search and graph memory?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains when semantic retrieval is enough and when relationship or temporal reasoning needs graph structure.

faq.question: Does it support temporal facts?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes valid-from and valid-until periods for facts and shows how to query memory at a point in time.

faq.question: Is the included vector embedding code production ready?
faq.answer: No. The sample uses placeholder random embeddings and should be replaced with a real embedding model.

faq.question: What privacy issues should users consider?
faq.answer: Users should define consent, retention, access control, deletion, and audit policies before storing personal or sensitive memories.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use this for implementation planning?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill gives design patterns and example structures that Claude Code or Codex can adapt to a codebase.
